perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, but questions of intellectual property ownership shall be governed by the laws of the state where the intellectual property was created.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Delaware law governs the entire agreement. The modification introduces a conflicting jurisdiction (state where IP was created) for intellectual property ownership, potentially leading to disputes over which state's laws apply.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Patent Law and choice of law principles
perturbation.law_explanation: While federal law governs patents, state law typically governs ownership disputes unless preempted. The change creates uncertainty and conflicts with standard choice-of-law principles as applied to contract law. State law usually defines IP ownership, and changing the choice of law mid-contract is contradictory.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982

perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that Delaware law governs the entire agreement. The changed text introduces a carve-out, specifying that the law of the state where intellectual property was created governs intellectual property ownership questions. This creates a direct conflict because it subjects a portion of the agreement to a different jurisdiction's laws, contradicting the initial choice of law provision. While the provided snippets primarily address ownership and assignment of patents under federal law (35 U.S.C. § 261), the Law Explanation correctly points out that state law governs ownership disputes unless preempted. This alteration undermines the original agreement's intent to have all matters governed by Delaware law, leading to potential legal uncertainty and disputes over which state's laws should apply. The introduction of another jurisdiction constitutes a contradiction of the original clause, fitting the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of Recipient under this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of two (2) years.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of Recipient under this Agreement shall survive termination at Discloser's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a definite survival period (2 years). The modification makes survival discretionary, creating ambiguity and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law requires reasonably certain terms.  Making the survival period indefinite and at the Discloser's discretion introduces uncertainty that could render the survival clause unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by introducing uncertainty regarding the survival period of the Recipient's obligations. The original text specifies a definite two-year period, while the changed text makes it discretionary at the Discloser's discretion. This introduces ambiguity, potentially rendering the survival clause unenforceable. While the provided snippets don't directly address survival clauses, the Law Explanation states, 'Contract law requires reasonably certain terms. Making the survival period indefinite and at the Discloser's discretion introduces uncertainty that could render the survival clause unenforceable.' This uncertainty contradicts the need for clear and definite terms in a contract, thus creating a legal contradiction as per the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to treat Confidential Information as confidential and proprietary, and to protect and preserve the confidential nature thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to treat Confidential Information as reasonably confidential, as the market dictates.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires treating confidential information as 'confidential and proprietary'. The modification introduces 'reasonably confidential, as the market dictates', weakening the obligation and potentially contradicting standard confidentiality definitions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret Law - Reasonable Efforts to Maintain Secrecy
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret law requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. Using 'reasonably confidential, as the market dictates' weakens the requirement, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard necessary to maintain trade secret protection. 'Market dictates' is too vague.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates strict confidentiality, while the changed text introduces a 'reasonably confidential' standard, contingent on 'market dictates.' This weakens the confidentiality obligation, which, as stated in the Law Explanation, may fail to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection, due to its vagueness. The misalignment of terminology, according to the definition, makes the contract potentially unenforceable. While there are no law snippets provided, the Law Explanation supports the idea that the altered language weakens the standard and may not be sufficient to maintain trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement reflects the parties' intentions on the date signed. Prior agreements shall be used as evidence of intent.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is an integration clause, stating this agreement is the complete agreement. The modification directly contradicts this by allowing prior agreements to be used as evidence, undermining the purpose of the integration clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The parol evidence rule generally prevents the introduction of prior agreements to contradict a fully integrated contract. Allowing prior agreements as evidence of intent directly violates this rule and creates ambiguity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: , from early projects like the restatement of basic legal subjects to a collaboration with NCCUSL in developing and monitoring the Uniform Commercial Code. View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. View more information about the Biddle Law Library Collections Explore the collection of The Report the Law School’s yearbook Explore the Penn Carey Law Oral History Project collection Contact & Hours The Archives and Special Collections office is located on the fourth floor of Tanenbaum Hall. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of desired research date. Visitors to the Law School will be asked to show identification. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. We do not transcribe, paraphrase, or otherwise interpret information for the researcher. Research requests generally take 2-3 weeks to complete, depending on the nature of the question and the number of outstanding requests at the time of submission. In addition, the Archives can only offer one hour of research assistance, patrons are asked to make an appointment for requests that will take longer than one hour to complete. Please also note that remote research requests are limited, and the Archives can only handle copying/scanning requests up to 20 individual documents. If a request exceeds this number, we suggest you schedule an appointment to visit the Archives in person. Handling Materials Wash hands before touching materials. Protective gloves may be required. Remove only one folder from a box at a time. Documents should be maintained in the exact order they are arranged in each file. If a document is out of order, notify the Archives staff. Make no marks or erasures on any document. Use strips provided to mark pages for copying. Eating or drinking is not permitted. Use pencils only, no pens or markers. Laptop
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a direct contradiction regarding the admissibility of prior agreements. The original text contains an integration clause, meant to prevent prior agreements from being considered. By stating 'Prior agreements shall be used as evidence of intent,' the changed text explicitly allows for the consideration of prior agreements, which contradicts the purpose of the integration clause, as the law explanation indicates.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that irreparable damage would occur if any provision of this Agreement were not performed in accordance with the terms hereof and that the non-breaching party shall be entitled to specific performance of the terms hereof, in addition to any other remedy to which it is entitled at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties believe damages would be the appropriate compensation if any provision of this Agreement were not performed, and the non-breaching party shall only be entitled to monetary damages.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for specific performance as a remedy. The modification removes specific performance and limits remedies to monetary damages, which contradicts the initial agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Availability of Specific Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: Specific performance is an available remedy when monetary damages are inadequate. The changed text stating only monetary damages are available directly contradicts this principle and limits the remedies available to the non-breaching party, potentially leaving them without adequate recourse.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement regarding available remedies. The original text allows for specific performance, while the changed text limits remedies to monetary damages only. The Wex snippet defines specific performance as a contractual remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract, especially when monetary damages are inadequate. By removing specific performance as an option, the changed text creates a legal contradiction, undermining the potential for the non-breaching party to receive what was originally agreed upon.
